Metadata.Coram: High Court — Suit Nos 107 of 2017, 80 of 2017, 337 of 2016 and 104 of 2016
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: MCH International Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: MCH International Pte Ltd is a company whose majority shareholder is Henry Wong Kok Hwee.

On 19 January 2015, MCH International Pte Ltd and YG Logistics Pte Ltd agreed key terms of a joint venture under which MCH would own 70% of the shares in a new joint venture company and YG Logistics would own 30%.

On 19 January 2015, it was agreed that YG Logistics Pte Ltd would extend a loan of up to S$4,000,000 to MCH International Pte Ltd to assist MCH with its portion of the purchase price for acquiring the Target Companies, with the first disbursement of S$500,000 in January 2015.
On 29 January 2015, MCH International Pte Ltd entered into a Shareholders Agreement with YG Group Pte Ltd and YG Logistics Pte Ltd relating to the joint venture company YG Group Pte Ltd.
On 29 January 2015, MCH International Pte Ltd entered into a Subscription Agreement with YG Group Pte Ltd and YG Logistics Pte Ltd for subscription of shares in YG Group Pte Ltd.
On 29 January 2015, MCH International Pte Ltd entered into a Call Option Agreement with YG Logistics Pte Ltd concerning shares in YG Group Pte Ltd.

On 29 January 2015, a Loan Agreement was executed under which YG Logistics Pte Ltd agreed to lend up to S$4,000,000 to MCH International Pte Ltd, with a provision that MCH would return all outstanding loan amounts if YG Logistics did not wish to proceed with the proposed acquisition of the Target Companies upon completion of due diligence.

On 29 January 2015, a Deed of Personal Guarantee was signed by Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Sing Lee Mee Yoke guaranteeing the payment obligations of MCH International Pte Ltd under the Loan Agreement with YG Logistics Pte Ltd.

On 18 August 2015, an Amendment Agreement in respect of the Shareholders Agreement was executed between YG Group Pte Ltd, YG Logistics Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd to reflect changes to the acquisition structure.
On 18 August 2015, an Amendment Agreement in respect of the Subscription Agreement was executed between YG Group Pte Ltd, YG Logistics Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd.
On 18 August 2015, an Amendment Agreement in respect of the Call Option Agreement was executed between YG Logistics Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd.
On 18 August 2015, an Amendment Agreement in respect of the Loan Agreement was executed between YG Logistics Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd, amending the total loan amount to S$450,000.

On 18 August 2015, a Side Deed in respect of the Deed of Personal Guarantee was executed relating to the guarantees given by Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Sing Lee Mee Yoke for MCH International Pte Ltd's obligations.

By 19 August 2015, the combined effect of the amended Loan Agreement and the Share Purchase Agreement was that the cash for YG Group Pte Ltd's purchase of the first 40% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd shares came entirely from YG Logistics Pte Ltd, with MCH International Pte Ltd as borrower under the loan.

On 28 July 2016, MCH International Pte Ltd stopped paying interest that had been due on the loan from YG Logistics Pte Ltd, stating that YG Logistics had disrupted its business and engineered a default on interest payment.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether MCH International Pte Ltd was contractually responsible for obligations in the Deed of Undertakings and whether events under the joint venture and loan structure triggered an event of default making the loan immediately payable.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Singapore contract law, a company is bound only by agreements to which it is a party or which are entered into on its behalf within the scope of authority, and loan agreements may provide that specified failures to comply with transaction documents or the institution of legal proceedings having a material financial effect constitute events of default that render all outstanding amounts immediately due and payable.
Party_Details.Application: MCH International Pte Ltd entered into the Shareholders Agreement, Subscription Agreement, Call Option Agreement and Loan Agreement, and later amendment agreements, as part of a joint venture financing structure under which YG Logistics Pte Ltd funded the first tranche of the acquisition of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd. The Deed of Undertakings was signed by Henry Wong Kok Hwee personally, while the Loan Agreement defined events of default by reference to MCH International Pte Ltd's performance under specified transaction documents and the financial impact of legal proceedings on MCH International Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analyses whether MCH International Pte Ltd was a party to the Deed of Undertakings, concluding that the obligations in that deed were personal to Mr Wong and not corporate obligations of MCH. It further considers whether breaches of the Deed of Undertakings could constitute an event of default under clause 12.1(a) of the amended Loan Agreement, finding that because MCH was not a party to that deed, such breaches did not fall within that event of default. The court then examines whether the commencement of Suit 104 against MCH triggered clause 12.1(e) of the Loan Agreement as an event of default, assessing the reasonableness of YG Logistics Pte Ltd's view that the proceedings had a material financial effect on MCH and whether the suit was commenced in bad faith. The reasoning also addresses arguments about promissory estoppel and collateral contract arising from the 28 January 2015 email in relation to clause 3.8 of the Deed of Share Charge, and whether the corporate veil of MCH should be pierced to treat it as liable for Mr Wong's undertakings.

Metadata.Coram: High Court — Suit Nos 107 of 2017, 80 of 2017, 337 of 2016 and 104 of 2016
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Wong Kok Hwee

Facts.Text: In 2013, Henry Wong Kok Hwee was introduced to the owners of the Target Companies, namely Lim Chee Kian, Cai Yong Cheng and Koh Chaik Ming.
In early 2014, Henry Wong Kok Hwee engaged in in-depth discussions with Lim Chee Kian to structure a trade sale of the Target Companies.

Pursuant to discussions between Henry Wong Kok Hwee and the Vendors, the Vendors incorporated Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd and transferred control of the Target Companies to that company for purposes of acquisition.
Sometime in November 2014, Henry Wong Kok Hwee presented a business plan to Simon Liong Chung Yee proposing the setting up of a joint venture company to acquire the Target Companies.

In November 2014, Henry Wong Kok Hwee prepared an information pack containing detailed profiles of Dennis Seen, Lou Lin and Maglin Chng, identified as industry veterans and key specialists in the cold chain logistics industry, and proposed that they form a core management team with him.

In late 2014, Henry Wong Kok Hwee introduced Simon Liong Chung Yee to Mas Iskandar, describing him as having extensive experience in the logistics field and as a suitable neutral director candidate for the proposed joint venture.

Between December 2014 and January 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Simon Liong Chung Yee negotiated terms of a joint venture, including a proposed S$4,000,000 loan to MCH International Pte Ltd to fund its initial contribution.
At a meeting on 19 January 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee explained to Simon Liong Chung Yee that he needed cash for his portion of the purchase price because his assets were tied up.

At the 19 January 2015 meeting, Henry Wong Kok Hwee stated that he would take the lead in hiring and forming the core management team, including himself, Dennis Seen, Lou Lin, Maglin Chng and Michael Ang Chee Siong, with the three industry veterans to be hired by MCH International Pte Ltd and seconded to the joint venture company.

At the 19 January 2015 meeting, Henry Wong Kok Hwee agreed that YG Logistics Pte Ltd would extend a loan of up to S$4,000,000 to MCH International Pte Ltd, with an initial S$500,000 disbursement to cover legal and financial due diligence expenses and deployment costs of the core management team.
On 27 January 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee was appointed managing director of YG Group Pte Ltd upon its incorporation.

On 27 January 2015, YG Group Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore for the purpose of acquiring Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd, with Henry Wong Kok Hwee as managing director and Simon Liong Chung Yee and Mas Iskandar as directors.

On 29 January 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee signed a Deed of Undertakings in favour of YG Logistics Pte Ltd and YG Group Pte Ltd, undertaking to use his best efforts to hire and establish a core management team and to procure that certain individuals be hired by MCH International Pte Ltd and seconded to YG Group Pte Ltd.

On 28 January 2015, in response to Henry Wong Kok Hwee's request to qualify clause 3.8 of the Deed of Share Charge, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed that although Henry Wong Kok Hwee's 70% shares and voting rights were pledged to YG Logistics Pte Ltd, he would not interfere with operations or obstruct Henry Wong Kok Hwee in discharging his duties and that he kept the voting rights to feel comfortable with the S$4,000,000 loan.

On 30 January 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee emailed Simon Liong Chung Yee stating that under the Deed of Undertakings, MCH International Pte Ltd pledged to assemble a top class team to manage and run the business and that these personnel would mostly be paid by MCH.

On 29 January 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee and his wife Sing Lee Mee Yoke signed a Deed of Personal Guarantee guaranteeing MCH International Pte Ltd's payment obligations under the Loan Agreement with YG Logistics Pte Ltd.

On 30 January 2015, a Deed of Share Charge was signed by YG Logistics Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd, dated 19 August 2015, granting YG Logistics Pte Ltd rights including voting rights over MCH's shares in YG Group Pte Ltd.
On 1 September 2015, the Deed of Share Charge over MCH International Pte Ltd's shares in YG Group Pte Ltd was registered with the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority of Singapore.

On 29 January 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee emailed Simon Liong Chung Yee, Mas Iskandar and the core management team a copy of the financial due diligence report for the Target Companies up to October 2014, prepared by Shanghai Certified Public Accountants, including an English translation.
By 30 March 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Lim Chee Kian had reached in-principle agreement on key terms of the acquisition of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd by YG Group Pte Ltd.
On 28 April 2015, a first draft of the Share Purchase Agreement between YG Group Pte Ltd and the Vendors of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd was sent to Henry Wong Kok Hwee.
Around late April 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee visited the Target Companies in Shanghai to examine their business operations.

In a WhatsApp conversation around late April 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee reported to Simon Liong Chung Yee, Mas Iskandar and Michael Ang Chee Siong that the Target Companies were performing well and expressed optimism about the business.

On 28 June 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee emailed Simon Liong Chung Yee a draft legal due diligence report on the Target Companies and stated that there were a few compliance issues but they were anticipated or not major.

On 23 July 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee arranged a meeting in Shanghai to discuss the terms of the acquisition and the findings in the first legal due diligence report, attended by himself, Simon Liong Chung Yee, Dennis Seen, Michael Ang Chee Siong, Lim Chee Kian, Cai Yong Cheng and Mao Li.
After the 23 July 2015 meeting in Shanghai, Henry Wong Kok Hwee told Simon Liong Chung Yee that the results of the legal due diligence were satisfactory.
Sometime in July 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee informed Simon Liong Chung Yee that private equity firm KV Asia Capital Pte Ltd had expressed interest in participating in the joint venture.

On 4 August 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee emailed Simon Liong Chung Yee a document titled 'Yong Gui Investment – Commission Letter 150730' that appeared to be a commission agreement between himself and the Vendors.

On 5 August 2015 at 1.12am, Henry Wong Kok Hwee emailed Simon Liong Chung Yee stating that he had deliberately forwarded the commission document to show it, that it had been offered, and that he would not be accepting it as it was in conflict of interest.

In August 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Simon Liong Chung Yee agreed that a further loan of S$500,000 would be made to MCH International Pte Ltd in exchange for YG Logistics Pte Ltd having the opportunity to subscribe to an additional 5% of shares in YG Group Pte Ltd, and that MCH would be allowed to draw down the full loan sum on 19 August 2015 to fund its contribution to the first 40% acquisition of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd.

On 19 August 2015, a Sale and Purchase Agreement was executed under which YG Group Pte Ltd purchased 40% of the shares in Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd from the Vendors for US$4,400,000 and obtained a call option to purchase the remaining 60% for US$6,600,000 by 19 August 2016.

On 19 August 2015, without informing Simon Liong Chung Yee, Henry Wong Kok Hwee signed a letter from Lim Chee Kian on behalf of the Vendors promising him a commission of US$380,000 in two tranches linked to YG Group Pte Ltd's purchase of 40% and then 100% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd shares.
Around 28 August 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee received US$300,000 pursuant to the commission letter linked to YG Group Pte Ltd's purchase of 40% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd shares.
On 19 August 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee signed two declarations of trust purporting to create trusts over shares in MCH International Pte Ltd in favour of Lim Chee Kian and Mao Li.

On 25 August 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Mas Iskandar proposing to amend the Memorandum and Articles of Association of YG Group Pte Ltd to align them with the Shareholders Agreement; Henry Wong Kok Hwee did not respond.

On 28 August 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee discovered that Simon Liong Chung Yee had amended the Sale and Purchase Agreement so that if the Vendors exercised their call option, the purchase money would be paid to YG Logistics Pte Ltd instead of YG Group Pte Ltd, and he expressed unhappiness about this change in email correspondence.
On 15 September 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee emailed Simon Liong Chung Yee requesting that due diligence expenses be shared, referring to earlier discussions about cost sharing.

On 3 October 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee emailed Simon Liong Chung Yee a Letter of Offer from KV Asia Capital Pte Ltd dated 2 October 2015 proposing capital injection into YG Group Pte Ltd in exchange for shares and involvement of additional logistics companies.

On 20 October 2015 at 11.26pm, Henry Wong Kok Hwee emailed Simon Liong Chung Yee stating that Simon's argument against the KV Asia proposal was one-sided, giving reasons why he considered the deal fair and listing instances where he felt Simon had not acted as a good partner.

On 6 November 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee reminding him that the parties had planned to exercise the Purchaser's Call Option around September or October 2015, later pushed to December 2015, and expressing a desire not to delay further or the deployment of the core management team.

On 19 November 2015, in a WhatsApp group conversation, Henry Wong Kok Hwee stated that unless asset tagging was done and he was comfortable with the business, the Purchaser's Call Option should not be exercised.

On 15 December 2015, Michael Ang Chee Siong began travelling across various cities in China to conduct asset tagging of the Target Companies, completing the exercise on 22 December 2015, with coordination from Henry Wong Kok Hwee.

On 21 December 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee urging him to procure the core management team to work in YG Group Pte Ltd and to avoid further delay in exercising the Purchaser's Call Option.

On 21 December 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee replied that his staff were on holiday but in constant communication with Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd on procurement of assets, scrapping of trucks, leasing terms and asset tagging, and stated that disputes over the KV Asia proposal and due diligence cost sharing had delayed preparations, and that he wanted clarity on final year financial numbers before exercising the Purchaser's Call Option.
On 26 January 2016, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee giving a deadline of 29 January 2016 to second the core management team to YG Group Pte Ltd.

On 16 February 2016, a YG Group Pte Ltd board meeting attended by Mas Iskandar, Henry Wong Kok Hwee, Simon Liong Chung Yee and Michael Ang Chee Siong discussed the timing of exercising the Purchaser's Call Option, with Henry Wong Kok Hwee stating that he wanted a second financial due diligence exercise to be completed before exercising the option.

In late February 2016, without informing Simon Liong Chung Yee, acquisition advisors Troy Shortell and Paraj Kakkar, who were associated with Henry Wong Kok Hwee, approached Yang Kee Logistics Pte Ltd to explore a potential acquisition of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd.

On 21 February 2016, Lim Chee Kian emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Simon Liong Chung Yee stating that six months had been lost in getting their team up to speed with daily operations and urging increased involvement in the operations of the company.

On 18 March 2016, YG Logistics Pte Ltd issued a notice purporting to exercise its rights under clause 3.8 of the Deed of Share Charge to appoint Bernard Tan Keng Beng as the proxy for MCH International Pte Ltd.

On 22 March 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd held an extraordinary general meeting where Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Mas Iskandar left after a dispute over whether Dennis Seen could attend as proxy for MCH International Pte Ltd, and from that point Bernard Tan Keng Beng voted on behalf of MCH at general meetings.

On 29 March 2016, Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Mas Iskandar held an extraordinary general meeting of YG Group Pte Ltd without attendance from YG Logistics Pte Ltd, describing it as a reconvened meeting from 22 March 2016.
On 15 April 2016, Mas Iskandar was removed as a director of YG Group Pte Ltd and replaced by Michael Ang Chee Siong.
On 19 April 2016, Bernard Tan Keng Beng was appointed as a director of YG Group Pte Ltd.

After 19 April 2016, Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Mas Iskandar filed a complaint with the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority of Singapore regarding the removals and appointments of directors in YG Group Pte Ltd.

After Mas Iskandar's removal as director of YG Group Pte Ltd on 15 April 2016, Henry Wong Kok Hwee continued to copy Mas Iskandar on email correspondence with Simon Liong Chung Yee relating to YG Group Pte Ltd, despite Simon's warnings about confidentiality provisions.
On 14 April 2016, a second financial due diligence report on the Target Companies was completed and circulated by Mao Li.
On 27 May 2016, Henry Wong Kok Hwee stated that based on the second financial due diligence report there were outstanding issues that justified delaying the exercise of the Purchaser's Call Option.

On 5 June 2016, Yang Kee Logistics Pte Ltd sent an offer by email to purchase 100% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd for US$17,500,000 following negotiations in which Henry Wong Kok Hwee participated through intermediary Troy Shortell.

On 14 July 2016, Yang Kee Logistics Pte Ltd sent a signed Letter of Intent offering to purchase 100% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd for US$15,500,000 after earlier indicating withdrawal due to concerns about meeting a Vendor and clarity of shareholding structure.

On 28 July 2016, Henry Wong Kok Hwee, on behalf of MCH International Pte Ltd, stopped paying interest on the loan from YG Logistics Pte Ltd, stating that YG Logistics had disrupted MCH's business and engineered a default on interest payment.

Sometime in July 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd commissioned a fresh legal due diligence on the Target Companies because Henry Wong Kok Hwee did not provide access to the final copy of the earlier legal due diligence report.

On 18 August 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd exercised the Purchaser's Call Option and purchased the remaining 60% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd shares from the Vendors, with funding coming entirely from YG Logistics Pte Ltd.

Prior to 18 August 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd arranged for Mao Li to remain involved in the Target Companies for an additional three months until November 2016 at a monthly compensation of RMB120,000 to assist with transition.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Henry Wong Kok Hwee's pre-dispute conduct in managing the joint venture, negotiating acquisition structures, handling due diligence, deploying or not deploying the proposed core management team, engaging with alternative investors and potential purchasers, and managing information flows created contractual or director-level exposure in relation to the joint venture companies and financing arrangements.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Singapore law, a person who signs a deed of undertakings may assume personal contractual obligations to use best efforts to procure specified actions, and a director of a company is generally act in the interests of the company, to exercise due care, skill and diligence, and to avoid undisclosed personal benefits or conflicts in transactions involving the company.
Party_Details.Application: Henry Wong Kok Hwee initiated and structured a joint venture to acquire the Target Companies, proposed and promoted a core management team, and personally signed a Deed of Undertakings to use best efforts to hire and second that team. He managed negotiations with the Vendors, controlled the flow of due diligence information, and influenced the timing of exercising the Purchaser's Call Option. He also entered into a commission arrangement and trust declarations with the Vendors on the same day as the Sale and Purchase Agreement, received a commission payment, and later engaged with KV Asia Capital Pte Ltd and Yang Kee Logistics Pte Ltd on potential transactions involving the Target Companies while YG Group Pte Ltd held an interest. These actions formed the factual basis for later disputes about whether he complied with his contractual undertakings and with standards applicable to his role in the joint venture companies.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment evaluates whether Henry Wong Kok Hwee breached obligations under the Deed of Undertakings and whether his conduct as managing director of YG Group Pte Ltd met the standards expected of a director. It interprets the phrase 'completion of the Acquisition' in the Deed of Undertakings by reference to the Sale and Purchase Agreement and surrounding documents, concluding that the obligation to procure hiring and secondment of the core management team arose upon the acquisition of 40% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd. The court considers contemporaneous communications, including emails and WhatsApp messages, to assess Henry Wong's understanding of his obligations and the timing of deployment of the core management team. It also examines the undisclosed commission letter and trust declarations, the handling of the KV Asia proposal, the delay in exercising the Purchaser's Call Option, the non-disclosure of the Yang Kee Logistics offers, and the continued copying of a former director on confidential communications, in determining whether he failed to act in the interests of YG Group Pte Ltd or to exercise due care, skill and diligence. The reasoning links these findings to remedies sought in Suit 80 and to the assessment of events of default and conspiracy allegations in the related suits.

Metadata.Coram: High Court — Suit Nos 107 of 2017, 80 of 2017, 337 of 2016 and 104 of 2016
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Sing Lee Mee Yoke

Facts.Text: As at 2015, Sing Lee Mee Yoke was the wife of Henry Wong Kok Hwee and a director of MCH International Pte Ltd.

On 29 January 2015, Sing Lee Mee Yoke signed a Deed of Personal Guarantee together with Henry Wong Kok Hwee, guaranteeing the payment obligations of MCH International Pte Ltd under the Loan Agreement with YG Logistics Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Sing Lee Mee Yoke, as guarantor and director associated with MCH International Pte Ltd, was exposed to liability under the loan and related joint venture arrangements.
Party_Details.Rule: A person who signs a personal guarantee generally undertakes to be responsible for the guaranteed party's payment obligations under the terms of the underlying loan agreement, the construction of the guarantee and any applicable defences.
Party_Details.Application: Sing Lee Mee Yoke, as majority shareholder's spouse and a director of MCH International Pte Ltd, executed a Deed of Personal Guarantee in favour of YG Logistics Pte Ltd, thereby linking her financial exposure to MCH International Pte Ltd's obligations under the Loan Agreement that funded the joint venture's acquisition of the Target Companies.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment considers the position of Sing Lee Mee Yoke as a guarantor of MCH International Pte Ltd's obligations under the Loan Agreement in Suit 337, including whether events of default occurred that would trigger liability under the guarantee. It also notes evidence that MCH International Pte Ltd was controlled by Henry Wong Kok Hwee and that Sing Lee Mee Yoke accepted this characterisation, but concludes that this control did not by itself justify piercing the corporate veil to impose additional obligations on MCH or on her beyond the express terms of the guarantee.

Metadata.Coram: High Court — Suit Nos 107 of 2017, 80 of 2017, 337 of 2016 and 104 of 2016
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: YG Group Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: YG Group Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 27 January 2015 as a joint venture vehicle to acquire Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd and the Target Companies in the cold chain logistics business.

On 27 January 2015, Henry Wong Kok Hwee was appointed managing director of YG Group Pte Ltd, and Simon Liong Chung Yee and Mas Iskandar were appointed as directors, with Mas Iskandar as chairman of the board.
At incorporation, the shareholders of YG Group Pte Ltd were YG Logistics Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd, with YG Logistics holding 30% and MCH holding 70% of the shares under the initial plan.
On 29 January 2015, YG Group Pte Ltd entered into a Shareholders Agreement with YG Logistics Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd governing their rights and obligations as shareholders.

On 29 January 2015, YG Group Pte Ltd entered into a Subscription Agreement with YG Logistics Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd for subscription of shares in YG Group Pte Ltd in tranches corresponding to acquisition payments for Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd.

On 18 August 2015, YG Group Pte Ltd executed amendment agreements to the Shareholders Agreement and Subscription Agreement to reflect a revised acquisition model involving a 40% initial purchase and a 60% call option.

On 19 August 2015, YG Group Pte Ltd executed a Sale and Purchase Agreement with the Vendors of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd to purchase 40% of its shares for US$4,400,000 and obtained a call option to purchase the remaining 60% for US$6,600,000 by 19 August 2016.

Under the 19 August 2015 Sale and Purchase Agreement, YG Group Pte Ltd obtained rights to nominate two directors to Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd, to nominate a bank signatory for payments above RMB10,000, and to nominate an understudy to the general manager, with the understudy's wages and accommodation to be borne by Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd.

For the first tranche of the acquisition, YG Group Pte Ltd was to receive subscription payments from MCH International Pte Ltd and YG Logistics Pte Ltd matching the US$4,400,000 due to the Vendors, with the cash for the first 40% purchase ultimately coming entirely from YG Logistics Pte Ltd.

On 23 July 2015, YG Group Pte Ltd representatives, including directors and proposed management, attended a meeting in Shanghai with the Vendors and the general manager of the Target Companies to discuss acquisition terms and legal due diligence findings.

On 16 February 2016, the board of YG Group Pte Ltd met and discussed whether to exercise the Purchaser's Call Option immediately or after completion of a second financial due diligence, with differing views among directors.
On 15 April 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd removed Mas Iskandar as a director and appointed Michael Ang Chee Siong as director.
On 19 April 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd appointed Bernard Tan Keng Beng as a director.
On 14 April 2016, a second financial due diligence report on the Target Companies commissioned by YG Group Pte Ltd was completed and circulated.
Sometime in July 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd commissioned a second legal due diligence on the Target Companies in preparation for exercising the Purchaser's Call Option.

On 18 August 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd exercised the Purchaser's Call Option and purchased the remaining 60% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd shares from the Vendors, funded entirely by YG Logistics Pte Ltd.

Before exercising the Purchaser's Call Option on 18 August 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd arranged for general manager Mao Li to remain involved in the Target Companies for an additional three months until November 2016 at a monthly compensation of RMB120,000.
On 29 August 2016, YG Group Pte Ltd revoked the appointment of Henry Wong Kok Hwee as managing director.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether YG Group Pte Ltd, as the joint venture vehicle, was affected by pre-dispute conduct relating to acquisition structuring, governance, deployment of management, and financing, and how these facts related to later claims about directors' conduct, events of default, conspiracy, and winding up.
Party_Details.Rule: A joint venture company is governed by its constitutional documents and shareholders' agreements, and its directors are manage it in accordance with those instruments and applicable company law, including proper exercise of options, adherence to agreed governance structures, and appropriate handling of due diligence and information flows.
Party_Details.Application: YG Group Pte Ltd was formed to acquire Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd and entered into a network of agreements with its shareholders to fund and structure the acquisition in two phases. Its board composition, extraordinary general meetings, commissioning of financial and legal due diligence, and eventual exercise of the Purchaser's Call Option were all shaped by the interactions between its two shareholder groups and their representatives. These pre-dispute events formed the factual background for later allegations about whether its governance had been altered improperly, whether its managing director had managed the acquisition process appropriately, and whether the circumstances justified relief such as winding up.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment uses the formation and operation of YG Group Pte Ltd to frame the disputes in all four suits. It analyses the Shareholders Agreement, Subscription Agreement, Sale and Purchase Agreement, and related amendments to determine how the acquisition of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd was intended to proceed and what rights YG Group Pte Ltd held at each stage. The court examines board and shareholder meetings, including the extraordinary general meetings in March 2016 and subsequent changes in directorships, to assess whether governance actions were consistent with the agreed framework. It also considers YG Group Pte Ltd's commissioning of financial and legal due diligence and its timing in exercising the Purchaser's Call Option in evaluating claims about directors' duties, alleged events of default, and whether there was any conspiracy or basis for winding up the company on just and equitable grounds.

Metadata.Coram: High Court — Suit Nos 107 of 2017, 80 of 2017, 337 of 2016 and 104 of 2016
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: YG Logistics Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: YG Logistics Pte Ltd is a company whose majority shareholder is Mactron Holdings Pte Ltd, an investment holding company in which Simon Liong Chung Yee is a shareholder and the sole director.
YG Logistics Pte Ltd was one of the two shareholders of YG Group Pte Ltd, holding a minority stake in the joint venture company alongside MCH International Pte Ltd.

On 19 January 2015, YG Logistics Pte Ltd agreed to extend a loan of up to S$4,000,000 to MCH International Pte Ltd to assist with MCH's portion of the purchase price for acquiring the Target Companies, with the first S$500,000 disbursement in January 2015.
On 29 January 2015, YG Logistics Pte Ltd entered into a Shareholders Agreement with YG Group Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd governing their joint venture.
On 29 January 2015, YG Logistics Pte Ltd entered into a Subscription Agreement with YG Group Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd for subscription of shares in YG Group Pte Ltd.
On 29 January 2015, YG Logistics Pte Ltd entered into a Call Option Agreement with MCH International Pte Ltd relating to shares in YG Group Pte Ltd.

On 29 January 2015, YG Logistics Pte Ltd entered into a Loan Agreement with MCH International Pte Ltd under which it agreed to lend up to S$4,000,000, with a clause requiring repayment of outstanding amounts if YG Logistics Pte Ltd did not wish to proceed with the acquisition after due diligence.

On 29 January 2015, YG Logistics Pte Ltd was a beneficiary of a Deed of Personal Guarantee signed by Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Sing Lee Mee Yoke guaranteeing MCH International Pte Ltd's obligations under the Loan Agreement.

On 30 January 2015, YG Logistics Pte Ltd and MCH International Pte Ltd signed a Deed of Share Charge, dated 19 August 2015, granting YG Logistics Pte Ltd rights including to exercise voting rights attached to MCH's shares in YG Group Pte Ltd.

On 28 January 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee, on behalf of YG Logistics Pte Ltd, emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee stating that although Henry's 70% shares and voting rights were pledged to YG Logistics Pte Ltd, he would not interfere with operations or obstruct Henry in discharging his duties and that he kept the voting rights to feel comfortable with the S$4,000,000 loan.

On 18 August 2015, YG Logistics Pte Ltd executed amendment agreements to the Shareholders Agreement, Subscription Agreement, Call Option Agreement and Loan Agreement to reflect the revised acquisition model and to amend the total loan amount to S$450,000.
By 19 August 2015, the cash used by YG Group Pte Ltd to purchase the first 40% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd shares came entirely from YG Logistics Pte Ltd under the loan and subscription structure.

On 25 August 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee, associated with YG Logistics Pte Ltd, emailed proposing amendments to YG Group Pte Ltd's Memorandum and Articles of Association to align them with the amended Shareholders Agreement.

On 20 October 2015 at 9.08pm, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed rejecting the KV Asia Capital Pte Ltd offer, stating that he considered the proposal disadvantageous to YG Logistics Pte Ltd and that he felt pressured by Henry Wong Kok Hwee to accept it.
On 6 November 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee expressing concern about delays in exercising the Purchaser's Call Option and the non-deployment of the core management team.
On 26 January 2016, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee giving a deadline of 29 January 2016 to second the core management team to YG Group Pte Ltd.

On 18 March 2016, YG Logistics Pte Ltd issued a notice purporting to exercise its rights under clause 3.8 of the Deed of Share Charge to appoint Bernard Tan Keng Beng as the proxy for MCH International Pte Ltd at YG Group Pte Ltd meetings.
On 18 August 2016, YG Logistics Pte Ltd provided the funding used by YG Group Pte Ltd to exercise the Purchaser's Call Option and purchase the remaining 60% of Yong Gui Investment Pte Ltd shares.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether YG Logistics Pte Ltd's role as lender and shareholder, including its exercise of rights under the Deed of Share Charge and its assessment of events of default, affected the loan's repayment status and its position in the joint venture.
Party_Details.Rule: A lender may define events of default in a loan agreement by reference to the borrower's compliance with specified transaction documents and the occurrence of legal proceedings having a material financial effect, and may secure its position through share charges that confer voting and other rights, any binding variations or estoppels.
Party_Details.Application: YG Logistics Pte Ltd funded MCH International Pte Ltd's participation in the joint venture through a loan and secured its position via a Deed of Share Charge and personal guarantees. It later sought to exercise voting rights under the share charge and to treat certain events, including non-compliance with the Deed of Undertakings and the commencement of litigation, as events of default under the amended Loan Agreement. These actions were grounded in the pre-dispute contractual framework and communications between the parties.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment examines YG Logistics Pte Ltd's reliance on clause 12.1 of the amended Loan Agreement to assert that various incidents constituted events of default, including alleged breaches of the Deed of Undertakings, the commencement of Suit 104, alleged breaches of the Deed of Share Charge and Shareholders Agreement, and non-payment of interest. It interprets the scope of clause 12.1(e) and whether it is limited to third-party proceedings, and assesses whether Suit 104 was commenced in bad faith. The court also analyses the effect of the 28 January 2015 email on YG Logistics Pte Ltd's ability to exercise voting rights under clause 3.8 of the Deed of Share Charge, considering arguments based on promissory estoppel and collateral contract, and ultimately determines which alleged events of default were made out and how they affected the loan's repayment obligations.

Metadata.Coram: High Court — Suit Nos 107 of 2017, 80 of 2017, 337 of 2016 and 104 of 2016
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Liong Chung Yee

Facts.Text: Simon Liong Chung Yee was a shareholder and the sole director of Mactron Holdings Pte Ltd, the majority shareholder of YG Logistics Pte Ltd.
On 27 January 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee was appointed a director of YG Group Pte Ltd.

Sometime in November 2014, Simon Liong Chung Yee received a business plan and information pack from Henry Wong Kok Hwee proposing a joint venture to acquire the Target Companies and describing a core management team.

Between December 2014 and January 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee negotiated with Henry Wong Kok Hwee regarding the joint venture structure, including a proposed S$4,000,000 loan to MCH International Pte Ltd and the composition of the core management team.

At the 19 January 2015 meeting, Simon Liong Chung Yee expressed that because the loan placed the financial risk of the venture on him, he required full confidence in the due diligence to be conducted on the Target Companies.

At the 19 January 2015 meeting, Henry Wong Kok Hwee reassured Simon Liong Chung Yee that he had 14 years of industry and mergers and acquisitions experience and that the firms chosen for financial and legal due diligence were the best in the market.

On 28 January 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee stating that although Henry's 70% shares and voting rights in YG Group Pte Ltd were pledged to YG Logistics Pte Ltd, he would not interfere with operations or obstruct Henry in discharging his duties and that he kept the voting rights to feel comfortable with the S$4,000,000 loan.

On 8 August 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee replied to Henry Wong Kok Hwee thanking him for his openness and integrity after Henry forwarded the commission letter and stated he would not accept the commission.

On 21 September 2015 at 11.37pm, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee explaining that in August they had amended seven signed agreements except the Deed of Undertakings due to time constraints and that he thought they needed to discuss how to implement the Deed of Undertakings under the revised 40%/60% acquisition model.

On 20 October 2015 at 9.08pm, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee rejecting the KV Asia Capital Pte Ltd offer, listing reasons why he considered it a bad deal for YG Logistics Pte Ltd and stating that he felt YG Logistics was being short changed while MCH International Pte Ltd would enjoy large premiums.

On 6 November 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee reminding him of the planned timing for exercising the Purchaser's Call Option and the expectation that the core management team would be involved in YG Group Pte Ltd's operations shortly after the Sale and Purchase Agreement.

On 21 December 2015, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee urging him to procure the core management team to work in YG Group Pte Ltd and to avoid further delay in exercising the Purchaser's Call Option.
On 26 January 2016, Simon Liong Chung Yee emailed Henry Wong Kok Hwee giving a deadline of 29 January 2016 to second the core management team to YG Group Pte Ltd.

On 16 February 2016, at a YG Group Pte Ltd board meeting, Simon Liong Chung Yee objected to delaying the exercise of the Purchaser's Call Option until after a second financial due diligence and expressed the view that the option should be exercised as soon as possible.

On 18 March 2016, Simon Liong Chung Yee, through YG Logistics Pte Ltd, issued a notice purporting to exercise rights under clause 3.8 of the Deed of Share Charge to appoint Bernard Tan Keng Beng as proxy for MCH International Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Simon Liong Chung Yee's pre-dispute conduct as a director and as the controlling mind behind YG Logistics Pte Ltd, including his communications and exercise of contractual rights, contributed to events of default or to any conspiracy or oppression.
Party_Details.Rule: A director and controlling shareholder involved in a joint venture is act in accordance with the joint venture agreements and may exercise contractual rights such as share charge voting powers and loan default provisions, any binding assurances, estoppels, or duties arising under company and contract law.
Party_Details.Application: Simon Liong Chung Yee negotiated the joint venture and loan structure, sought assurances about due diligence, and later pressed for deployment of the core management team and timely exercise of the Purchaser's Call Option. He communicated that he would not interfere with Henry Wong Kok Hwee's operational role despite holding voting rights under the share charge, and later caused YG Logistics Pte Ltd to exercise those rights by appointing a proxy. These actions formed part of the factual matrix for later claims that he and related entities had engineered events of default or engaged in a conspiracy, as well as for arguments about promissory estoppel and the scope of the share charge.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment considers Simon Liong Chung Yee's role in structuring the joint venture and loan, his insistence on due diligence, and his reliance on the Deed of Undertakings to secure the deployment of the core management team. It analyses his 28 January 2015 email in the context of Henry Wong's request to limit clause 3.8 of the Deed of Share Charge, concluding that the email did not amount to a clear and unequivocal promise restricting the exercise of voting rights only upon an event of default. The court also evaluates whether his decision to commence Suit 104 and to treat it as an event of default under the Loan Agreement was reasonable or in bad faith, and assesses allegations that he and associated entities engaged in lawful or unlawful conspiracy against MCH International Pte Ltd, Henry Wong Kok Hwee and Sing Lee Mee Yoke.
